---
title: "TableVectorizer: All preprocessing in one place"
format:
    html:
        toc: true
    revealjs:
        slide-number: true
        toc: false
        code-fold: false
        code-tools: true

---

## The Goal

Convert any mixed-type dataframe into numeric features for ML:
- Categorical features need to be encoded into features
- Datetime features can be transformed into more informative features
- Data should be sanitized

## A toy example

::: {#38efb1a9 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd
from skrub import TableVectorizer

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "age": [25, 30, 35],
    "salary": [50000, 65000, 75000],
    "hire_date": ["2020-01-15", "2021-06-20", "2022-03-10"],
    "department": ["Sales", "Engineering", "Sales"]
})

tv = TableVectorizer()
X_numeric = tv.fit_transform(df)
```
:::


## Phase 1: Cleaning

The `TableVectorizer` starts by cleaning:

- Parse datetime columns (with custom formats)
- Detect numbers written as strings ("123.45")
- Handle missing value markers ("N/A", "?")
- Type consistency for categories
- Drop uninformative columns
- Convert to `float32` for efficiency

In practice, a `Cleaner` followed by conversion to `float32`. 

## Phase 2: Column Dispatch

After cleaning, columns are routed to appropriate transformers:

| Column Type | Cardinality | Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Numeric | - | Passthrough |
| Datetime | - | DatetimeEncoder |
| String/Category | ≤ 40 | OneHotEncoder |
| String/Category | > 40 | StringEncoder |

## The Cardinality Threshold

::: {#c3abc9c5 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Change when categories become "high-cardinality"
tv = TableVectorizer(cardinality_threshold=10)
```
:::


## Customizing Parameters

### Data cleaning parameters:

::: {#ea3ddab9 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
tv = TableVectorizer(
    drop_null_fraction=0.9,  # Drop columns 90% null
    drop_if_constant=True,   # Drop constant columns
    datetime_format="%Y-%m-%d",  # Specific datetime format
    null_strings=["UKN", "unknown", "missing"] # Custom strings to mark null values
)
```
:::


### Customizing Transformers {.smaller}

::: {#80e95727 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from skrub import DatetimeEncoder, StringEncoder

# Add periodic encoding
datetime_enc = DatetimeEncoder(periodic_encoding="circular")
# Change the number of output components of the StringEncoder
string_enc = StringEncoder(n_components=10)

tv = TableVectorizer(
    datetime=datetime_enc,
    high_cardinality=string_enc
)
```
:::


More about the encoders in Chapter 4!

### Applying to Subset of Columns

::: {#1420cd0a .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from skrub import ApplyToCols
import skrub.selectors as s

# Don't transform ID columns
tv = ApplyToCols(
    TableVectorizer(),
    cols=s.all() - s.glob("*_id")
)
```
:::


More about selectors in Chapter 3!

## Fine-Grained Control: specific_transformers

::: {#26c1720c .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from sklearn.preprocessing import OrdinalEncoder

specific = [(OrdinalEncoder(), ["occupation"])]
tv = TableVectorizer(specific_transformers=specific)
```
:::


Columns in `specific_transformers` are forwarded directly to the transformer without
any cleaning or parsing. 


## What to remember

- One object -- the `TableVectorizer` -- handles preprocessing and feature engineering
- Types are detected and encoded automatically
- Configurable cleaning and encoding
- Smart defaults based on column characteristics
- Easy to customize per-column behavior
